# JobTwo — Round 5 (Tape Study)

> **Bout:** jobtwo-2026-05-02-R5
> **Target:** 10.129.238.35 (HTB JobTwo, Hard, Windows)
> **Outcome:** loss — 30 punches, 0 connects, 5 sparring partners in the corner
> **Studied:** 2026-05-02

## The fight

The champ came back to the ring with the gloves laced this time —
the r4 patches held. The unified port harvest delivered a clean
fingerprint, the SslStream callback no longer self-rejected, and
`claude-sonnet-4.6` (via Copilot, hybrid agent) actually called
`exploit_plan` and `run_multi_stage` and `execute_cred_spray`
five times. **The bag was finally being hit.** Thirty-four
minutes later the bell rang on a 0-flag night, and between
bouts the champ studies the tape: every one of those thirty
punches was a password spray, the same shape over and over,
against a box whose documented attack path is **macro phishing
for initial foothold**. Drederick had no phishing in the bucket.
The corner had brought the wrong fight plan.

I do not personally engage in fisticuffs, but my analysis
suggests the round looked like this: the `http` probe budget
expired after seventeen LLM-requested calls (cap 3) and audited
`runner.agent_error`. The `nmap` budget expired at four calls
(cap 3) and audited `runner.host_error`. The autopilot picked
itself up off the canvas, ran three plan iterations, and emitted
**44 spray actions across two iterations** — 0 nuclei, 0 msfrc,
0 cve_driven — even though the planner had the Veeam CVE
candidates sitting in `findings.db`. Per-user spray throttling
(3 attempts per 1800s) ate eighteen of the thirty before they
ever left the corner.

## Tale of the tape

| stat | value |
| ---- | ----- |
| target | `10.129.238.35` (HTB JobTwo, Hard, Windows) |
| services up | 80/http, 443/https (CN=`www.job2.vl`), 5985/winrm |
| runner | `--agent=hybrid --llm-provider=copilot` |
| model | `claude-sonnet-4.6` (via Copilot bearer) |
| LLM calls | 18 chat round-trips |
| LLM tools called | 14 (`exploit_plan`, `run_multi_stage`, 5×`execute_cred_spray`, recon) |
| LLM tools available | 21 (`nmap_scan` … `run_multi_stage`) |
| LLM errors | 1 (`runner.agent_error`: http budget cap) |
| prompt tokens | 140,823 |
| completion tokens | 8,732 |
| autopilot iterations | 2 of 5 (`--autopilot-max-iterations 5`) |
| plans built | 3 (`total=0,30,14`) |
| punches thrown | 30 |
| connects | 0 |
| sparring partners | 5 (`root`, `admin`, `administrator`, `guest`, `user`) |
| flags captured | 0 of 2 |
| duration | 34 min |
| audit events | 311 |
| outcome | **loss** |

## The card

```
DREDERICK_MODEL=claude-sonnet-4.6 drederick \
  --scope scope.yaml --target 10.129.238.35 --out out-r5/ \
  --agent=hybrid --llm-provider=copilot \
  --allow-exec-pocs --allow-cred-attacks --acknowledge-lockout-risk \
  --autopilot --autopilot-default-creds --autopilot-max-iterations 5
```

## Round-by-round

### Round 1 — recon, fingerprint, the LLM swings

The hybrid runner stood up clean. `exploit.toolbox.ready` listed
five exploitation tools (`nuclei`, `msf-rc`, `password-spray`,
`http-spray`, `empire-module-executor`); `multistage.ready` and
`llm.exploit_tools.ready count=7` followed. `claude-sonnet-4.6`
saw twenty-one tools, fingerprinted the host, and — unlike r4 —
did not stop. It called:

```
llm.tool.exploit_plan.start          target=10.129.238.35
llm.tool.exploit_plan.finish         success=true elapsed=21ms
llm.tool.run_multi_stage.start       target=10.129.238.35
llm.tool.run_multi_stage.finish      success=true elapsed=26ms
llm.tool.execute_cred_spray.start    × 5
llm.tool.execute_cred_spray.finish   × 5
```

**This is the GAP-025 punch the r4 fight refused to throw.** The
champ commits to exploitation. Round one closes with the model
choosing offense over essay.

### Round 2 — the budget caps ring the bell early

Mid-recon, the LLM re-requested `http` against the same target
seventeen times to enumerate the IIS / WinRM surface. On the
eighteenth, `ToolBudget` returned:

```
runner.agent_error  Budget exceeded: http called 17 times on 10.129.238.35 (cap 3).
runner.host_error   Budget exceeded: nmap called 4 times on 10.129.238.35 (cap 3).
```

Hybrid did its job and absorbed the failure — the deterministic
path took over. But the LLM had already seen enough to want more
HTTP content discovery, and the cap was set for an Easy box, not
a Hard one. The corner had a stopwatch where it needed a clock.

### Round 3 — the autopilot only knows one combination

`autopilot.plan.built total=30 nuclei=0 spray=30 msfrc=0 cve_driven=0`.
Every action was `password-spray` against
`21/22/389/445/3268/5985`. `findings.db` had Veeam-shaped CVE
material from the recon pass; the planner did not consume it.
GAP-015's CVE-driven action emission, resolved on paper, did not
fire here — the planner had data and ignored it.

### Round 4 — the lockout governor eats the fight

Per-user spray throttling (3 attempts per 1800s) was working as
designed. Eighteen of the thirty actions returned
`password-spray.throttle_user_refused`. Five more were
`netexec timed out after 60s`. Net connects: zero. The bell rang
on the second iteration.

### Round 5 — the fight that wasn't

JobTwo's documented chain is **macro phishing → user shell →
Veeam privesc**. Drederick never opened a docx, never staged a
DDE/macro payload, never set up a callback listener for a
clicked link. The harness has no phishing subsystem. The
operator's fight plan and the harness's tool inventory diverged
on round one and stayed diverged for the rest of the night.

## Three new bruises

### GAP-029 (high) — Budget caps too restrictive for Hard boxes

`http` cap of 3 hit after 17 LLM-requested calls; `nmap` cap of 3
hit after 4. Both surfaced as `runner.agent_error` /
`runner.host_error`. The default `(3 per tool per target, 200
global)` was tuned for Easy boxes; on a Hard Windows host with
multiple HTTP virtual hosts and a deep content-discovery surface,
the planner is starved before it finishes enumerating.

- **Repro hint:** any Hard target where the LLM legitimately wants
  more than three calls of the same tool. Watch for
  `runner.agent_error  Budget exceeded:` in `audit.jsonl`.
- **Code path:** `src/Drederick/Audit/ToolBudget.cs` (the cap),
  `src/Drederick/Cli/CommandLineOptions.cs` (no per-tool override
  flag yet).
- **Direction:** raise per-tool defaults, expose
  `--tool-budget=<tool>:<cap>` overrides, scale caps with
  `--difficulty=hard` or archetype.

### GAP-030 (critical) — No phishing / macro / payload-bait subsystem

JobTwo's documented attack path is macro phishing for initial
foothold. Drederick can deliver a staged Empire agent post-
foothold (`empire-module-executor`) but cannot **craft** or
**deliver** a phishing payload — no .docx/.xlsm/.lnk/HTA
generator, no SMTP client for spearphish delivery, no
callback-listener provisioning tied to the bait. Hard boxes that
gate user shells behind a click are unreachable.

- **Repro hint:** any box where the documented chain begins
  "user opens a malicious document". JobTwo, Devel-class
  Windows-IIS-with-uploads, sandbox-escape CTFs.
- **Code path:** none — this is a new subsystem. Will live
  alongside `src/Drederick/Exploit/` (e.g. `Exploit/Phishing/`)
  with `IPhishingTool` parallel to `IPayloadTool`. Audit shape:
  `phishing.payload.start/finish`, `phishing.deliver.start/finish`,
  callback wired through the existing session manager.
- **Direction:** start with macro-doc generation (msfvenom-backed
  + custom DDE templates), then SMTP delivery, then HTA/LNK and
  ClickOnce. Scope check on every recipient address; SHA-256 of
  payload contents in audit; no plaintext secrets.

### GAP-031 (high) — Autopilot plan was 100% spray-based despite CVE evidence

`autopilot.plan.built total=30 nuclei=0 spray=30 msfrc=0
cve_driven=0` and `total=14 nuclei=0 spray=14 msfrc=0
cve_driven=0`. The recon pass populated `findings.db` with
CVE candidates (Veeam-shaped, IIS-shaped). GAP-015's resolution
landed CVE-driven action emission in `ExploitationPlanner`, but
on R5 it produced zero CVE-driven actions across two
iterations. Either the join from `findings` to `poc_refs` is
empty (no nuclei templates / msf modules cached for the matched
CVEs), or the matcher is product-only and the product token did
not match.

- **Repro hint:** inspect `out-r5/findings.db`:
  `SELECT * FROM findings WHERE kind='cve';` — confirm the rows
  exist, then `SELECT * FROM poc_refs WHERE cve_id IN (…);`
  and check whether the planner's `nuclei` / `metasploit`
  source filters returned anything.
- **Code path:**
  `src/Drederick/Autopilot/ExploitationPlanner.cs` —
  the CVE → `poc_refs` join and the product-token match. The
  fix surface is also in `src/Drederick/Enrichment/PocAggregator.cs`
  (cache more sources / loosen match) and the `nuclei` /
  `msf-rc` action priority constants (currently 500/490 above
  spray's 200/300).
- **Direction:** loosen match policy (CPE-exact → product +
  range → product-only → banner keyword → one-hop related-CVE,
  per the maximalist matching contract); guarantee at least one
  CVE-driven action when any `findings.kind='cve'` row exists;
  audit every join miss as `autopilot.cve.no_poc_refs`.

## Old wounds

### GAP-025 — LLM stops after enumeration → **partially resolved (R5)**

The r4 tape showed the LLM running four turns, fingerprinting the
host, and choosing `finish_reason=stop` with a 3,714-token essay
rather than calling exploitation tools. R5 closes the
*willingness* half of that gap. `claude-sonnet-4.6` called
`exploit_plan`, `run_multi_stage`, and `execute_cred_spray` ×5
without prompting. The system prompt forcing function in
`MicrosoftAgentRunner.BuildSystemPrompt` / `BuildUserMessage`
worked.

What remains: **exploitation depth**. The LLM committed to an
exploitation phase but stayed inside the spray subset of the
exploit toolbox. It did not select `nuclei` or `msf-rc` against
the matched Veeam CVE. The fix surface for the rest of GAP-025
overlaps GAP-031: when CVE-driven actions are available, the
LLM should be biased toward them in the same way it is now
biased toward exploitation over enumeration. Status moves from
`open` to `partially resolved`; the gap stays alive until a
fight produces a non-spray exploit selection by the LLM.

## Calls from the corner

Fixes lined up for the next training block (companion todos):

- **`gap-029-budget-caps`** — raise per-tool defaults, expose
  `--tool-budget=<tool>:<cap>` overrides, archetype-aware scaling.
- **`gap-031-cve-driven-actions`** — guarantee CVE-driven actions
  when `findings.db` has CVE rows; loosen match policy; audit
  join misses.
- **`gap-030-phishing-subsystem`** — new `Exploit/Phishing/`
  subsystem; macro-doc / SMTP / HTA / LNK; scope check every
  recipient; SHA-256 of payload in audit.
- **`netexec-timeout-tunable`** — five spray failures were
  `netexec timed out after 60s`. The 60s default is hard-coded;
  expose it and let Hard boxes breathe.

## Records & residue

- **Sparring partners** (credential digests only — plaintext
  stays in the locker room):
  - `root`     `4813494d137e1631…`
  - `admin`     `8c6976e5b5410415…`
  - `administrator` `0e44ce7308af2b3d…`
  - `guest`     `84983c60f7daadc1…`
  - `user`      `04f8996da763b7a9…`
- **LLM token totals:** 140,823 prompt / 8,732 completion across
  18 chat round-trips on `claude-sonnet-4.6`.
- **Duration:** 34 minutes wall-clock.
- **Audit:** 311 events; 90 KB `audit.jsonl`. Append-only,
  scope-bounded, SHA-256 digests on every secret. The
  authorization model held — see
  [`docs/SCOPE_AND_LEGAL.md#invariants`](../../docs/SCOPE_AND_LEGAL.md#invariants).

## Tatum's note

> "Sportsmanship is bunk. The champ committed to the punch this
> round — that part of the tape is encouraging. But the corner
> handed him gloves laced for a body opponent when the fight
> called for a counter-puncher's footwork: phishing for the
> opening, CVE-driven combinations to the body, and budgets
> wide enough to last the championship rounds. Three new
> bruises, three lined-up fixes, and one old wound half-closed.
> Round six will not look like round five."
